not really. Python 2 bytes is an alias to str. So if you get 1 byte it will generate something like '[87]' (length 4 str).

===Python 3====
>>> byteArr = [23,67,75,254]
>>> bytes(byteArr)
b'\x17CK\xfe'
>>> len(bytes(byteArr))
4

===Python 2====
>>> byteArr = [23,67,75,254]
>>> bytes(byteArr)
'[23, 67, 75, 254]'
>>> len(bytes(byteArr))
17

>>> bytearray(byteArr)
bytearray(b'\x17CK\xfe')
>>> len(bytearray(byteArr))
4

The dollar-sign form for proxied-values has returned :)

My problem with the dollar-sign, even though it makes the most intuitive sense, is that is a bit error-prone. If a user mistakenly leaves off single-quotes when doing a `heroku config:set`, then they'll end up setting their config-variable to an empty string.

Later on, when they try to figure out why their app is not working, they'll be presented with an empty-string and be thoroughly confused--as I was when this happened.

I decided to avoid the issue entirely and use braces so we avoid the issue with bash escaping entirely. It's slightly less intuitive in the beginning, but never having to worry about bash-escaping again is a Good Thing (tm)!

- get input arrays (outer for loop)

Maybe for that case. But generally you _are_  trying to render a particular codeblock into the documentation, and that case will break constantly in the context of lines.

Again, maybe for this case. But if I'm trying to render a method body (for example), or a target definition from a BUILD file, edits before those lines in the file would silently break my `inc`.

There are very specific cases where lines are ok, but in cases where comments are legal, they should be used.

Given the philosophy that Pants generally does not modify files, (with the exception of `fmt` goal, but it does not modify BUILD files), it would be good to warn users not to mix this goal with other operations.

n [v0.2.4](https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/blob/41f800bcdaa403c5df4497e8690b974166857735/trackpy/tests/test_feature.py), the Gaussian is $e^{-(x^2+y^2)/(ndim*rg^2)}$
in which x, y are the meshgrids of coordinates and rg the absolute Rg.

If I write down the integrals for Rg of the general Gaussian $e^{-a r^2}$, I end up with $a = ndim / (2 *rg^2)$. So the factor in the v0.2 Gaussian is wrong. > It reads one message at a time off of the queue

Unavoidable, unfortunately.  To ensure multiple messages for a given app are stored in the same order they were sent (the same order in which the app produced them), messages have to be handled sequentially.

> and then locks at least three mutex objects

It's not actually that bad.  The locks acquired on adapters and drains are only read locks to ensure those components aren't nilled out by the configurer after we've checked that they aren't already nil.  Read locks aren't deleterious to performance in the way a write lock is since you rarely block waiting for a read lock.  As for the mutexes used within the storage adapters, we always check whether we need a lock before we bother to get one.  The conditions where one is needed are the first message for a new app or the destruction of logs for an app that's also been destroyed.  Both of these are relatively rare events.

> and the buffer will fill quickly. Then your sending process will begin to block...

The write to the internal channel is non-blocking.  If the buffer that is that channel fills up, messages are dropped right there so the UDP listener's own queue doesn't fill up.

> increasing channel buffer size

Bumped to 1000 per your recommendation.

> writing to an intermediate in-memory cache

That's the point of the internal channel / queue, so this is already being done.

I suspect the parser should be more complicated.
```
The header field value can be
   defined in ABNF syntax as:

       Forwarded   = 1#forwarded-element

       forwarded-element =
           [ forwarded-pair ] *( ";" [ forwarded-pair ] )

       forwarded-pair = token "=" value
       value          = token / quoted-string

       token = <Defined in [RFC7230], Section 3.2.6>
       quoted-string = <Defined in [RFC7230], Section 3.2.6>
```
It means the `forwarded-pair` elements could be in random order: `by=a; host=b.com` and `host=b.com, by=a` are both valid combinations.
Also value could be optionally quoted, aiohttp shoud unquote it automatically. 
pair-name is case-insensitive: all 'Host', 'host', and 'HOst` are valid names:
```
       Forwarded: for="_gazonk"
       Forwarded: For="[2001:db8:cafe::17]:4711"
       Forwarded: for=192.0.2.60;proto=http;by=203.0.113.43
       Forwarded: for=192.0.2.43, for=198.51.100.17

   Note that as ":" and "[]" are not valid characters in "token", IPv6
   addresses are written as "quoted-string".
```

OK here is the problem in step by step detail.

I want to be able to run the following on all IGangaFile objects to get my file at some destination:
```
inputFile.localDir = tmpDir
inputFile.get()
```

I expect from an API that I get __ALL__ of the files in the tmpDir.

This will __NOT__ work with `LocalFile` due to the fact that `localDir` is used to point to the file in the first place.

Adding an exception for the `LocalFile` is against the whole point of this PR and means that we have some _funny_ behaviour with one of the objects which all share this same interface which just confuses me let alone future developers.

Using:
```
inputFile.get(localPath=tmpDir)
```

Allows the file regardless of the type to transfer a file here.
Allowing an optional parameter doesn't effect the default behaviour of the `get()` method being called and simply adds extra functionality.

A reasonable argument is that the file object should then have it's `localDir` replaced with the contents of `tmpdir`. In the case of `LocalFile` this represents a move command which is potentially dangerous so we shouldn't delete a user's file on them which raises the question of what is proper here?

Another possible solution is:

```
inputFile.transfer(localPath=tmpDir)
```

Which allows the `get()` functionality to be untouched but still leaves us with a question of which file is the LocalFile at this point?
Obviously different developers have valid opinions on this but ultimately the user pointed us to 1 file which we're copying for them.
We either lose information about 1 of the locations or expand the schema to allow this file to have multiple locations on disk which seems counter-intuitive.


Another solution is to yes change the `LocalFile` schema so that `localDir` is always only the place where the file will end up after `get()` and will be before `put()`.
We should really be using something like `location` for the construction of `LocalFile` in order to do this but this is an invasive possibly completely incompatible change.

I believe best practice for Dockerfiles is to combine stuff into one big command and remove the temporary files, e.g., something like the following (taken from https://github.com/openai/gym/blob/master/Dockerfile)

```
RUN apt-get update \
    && apt-get install -y libav-tools \
    python-numpy \
    python-scipy \
    python-pyglet \
    python-setuptools \
    libpq-dev \
    libjpeg-dev \
    curl \
    cmake \
    swig \
    python-opengl \
    libboost-all-dev \
    libsdl2-dev \
    wget \
    unzip \
    git \
    xpra \
    && apt-get clean \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* \
    && easy_install pip
```
